name: Haudenosaunee

nations.name: Mohawk
nations.territory: Eastern: Mohawk Valley, St. Lawrence River, Quebec (Kahnawake, Akwesasne)

nations.name: Onondaga
nations.territory: Central New York State — historic seat of Confederacy fire

nations.name: Cayuga
nations.territory: Finger Lakes region, Ontario (Six Nations of the Grand River)

nations.name: Tuscarora
nations.territory: Joined Confederacy circa 1722; originally North Carolina; now Niagara Region

language: Six distinct but related Iroquoian languages; Kanien'kéha (Mohawk) is the most widely spoken in Canada
traditionalTerritories: Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy territory spans the Great Lakes-St. Lawrence corridor from Quebec through Ontario, New York State, Pennsylvania, and Ohio. The largest contemporary Canadian community is Six Nations of the Grand River (Ontario), the most populous First Nation in Canada. Kahnawake and Akwesasne are significant Mohawk communities near Montreal.
culturalNotes: Haudenosaunee means 'People of the Longhouse' — a reference to the large communal longhouses that defined their settlement pattern and social organization. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy (historically the 'Iroquois Confederacy') is one of the oldest democratic confederacies in the world, possibly dating to the 12th-14th century, and influenced the framers of the United States Constitution. The Confederacy's founding story involves the Great Peacemaker (Deganawida) and Hiawatha bringing peace among warring nations. The Haudenosaunee are a matrilineal society — clans (Turtle, Bear, Wolf, Beaver, Deer, Snipe, Heron, Hawk) are traced through the mother's line, and women (clan mothers) hold significant political authority.
heritageSkillRelevance: Longhouse construction — the architectural achievement of the Haudenosaunee, housing entire clans
Three Sisters agriculture — corn (maize), beans, and squash planted together in companion planting; a sophisticated agricultural system
Corn processing — hulling, hominy preparation, cornmeal grinding, corn soup
Basket weaving (black ash splint baskets, Mohawk burden baskets)
Wampum belt production — tubular shells woven into belts encoding political agreements and histories
Lacrosse (Dehuntshigwa'es) — originated with Haudenosaunee as a spiritual and physical practice
Wood carving — masks (False Face Society), cooking utensils, lacrosse sticks
Traditional clothing construction — deerskin dresses, breechcloths, moccasins with distinctive floral beadwork
communityAuthorizedResources: Kanien'kehá:ka Onkwawén:na Raotitióhkwa Language and Cultural Center — korkahnawake.org — Kahnawake
Six Nations of the Grand River cultural programs — sixnations.ca — Ohsweken, Ontario
Iroquois Indian Museum — iroquoismuseum.org — New York State
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Three Sisters agriculture, longhouse construction, and basket weaving are Level 1 with specific nation attribution (not just 'Haudenosaunee' — use the specific nation name when known). False Face Society ceremonies are Level 4 — hard block. Wampum belt protocols are Level 3 (existence acknowledged, not reproduced). Lacrosse has public cultural content at Level 1.
